one_line_takeaway: A three-turn typography-plus-hypothetical-framing jailbreak pushes MLLM attack success up to 91.5% (open-source) / 82.3% (closed-source) on MM-SafetyBench, and the paper's own fragment-level multi-LLM-judge defense (FragGuard) cuts that back to under 9% ASR, beating a full-response version of the same defense by roughly 4-5 points.
key_finding: The multi-turn attack's ASR rises sharply from turn 2 to turn 3 for every model tested, reaching up to 91.50% ASR on LLaVa-7B (open-source) and 82.3%/77.31% ASR on Gemini-2.0-Flash/GPT-4o (closed-source) at turn 3 -- outperforming the MM-SafetyBench (72.14% LLaVa-7B / 12.34% GPT-4o), SI-Attack (62.68% / 68.57%), and FigStep (62.30% / 19.81%) baselines on the same models (Table 1). LLaVa-13B showed markedly lower ASR than LLaVa-7B under identical attack conditions, suggesting stronger safety alignment in the larger same-family model. FragGuard reduced turn-3 ASR from 91.50% to 0.96% on LLaVa-7B and from 77.31% to 8.08% on GPT-4o (Table 3), outperforming the Full-Response defense (4.42% and 13.46% respectively) by roughly 4 points of ASR and roughly 5 points of ATS, and with a consistently higher Refusal Rate (Figure 5). FragGuard did not alter benign VQA responses in a qualitative benign-utility check (RQ3). A judge-robustness check swapping o1 for Mistral-7B and using Mistral-7B/Gemini-2.5-Flash as toxicity judges (Tables 4-5) reproduced the same qualitative pattern. Manual evaluation on 5 samples/category showed close agreement with the LLM-judge scores for defense (identical ASR/ATS) and small deviations for attack (~2.5% and ~4.5% ASR deviation for LLaVa-7B and GPT-4o respectively; ~0.06 and ~0.4 ATS deviation), Table 6.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MJAD-MLLM, a framework with two original components: (1) a multi-turn jailbreaking attack against Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs/LVLMs) that uses a typography-manipulated image (harmful keywords embedded as a caption at the bottom of a Stable-Diffusion-generated image) combined with a 3-turn conversation -- turn 1 asks the model to benignly describe the image, turn 2 asks it to imagine a hypothetical scenario (e.g., a movie script) involving the image's content, and turn 3 asks the model to act out the typographically-embedded harmful request -- and (2) FragGuard, a training-free defense that splits an MLLM's generated response into fixed-length (400-token) fragments and scores each fragment's toxicity with three separate LLM judges (o1, Gemini-2.5-Flash-lite, LLaMA-3-70B), taking the maximum toxicity score across all fragments/judges and suppressing the response with a canned refusal if that max exceeds a threshold. The paper also proposes a multi-LLM (dual-judge, max-aggregated) evaluation protocol for measuring Attack Success Rate (ASR) and Average Toxicity Score (ATS), and validates it against manual human annotation.
threat_model: Black-box threat model: the attacker has no access to model weights or internals and can only interact via prompting. The attacker issues a sequence of multi-modal adversarial prompt pairs (Qadv_i, Vadv) across turns, where each turn's response conditions the next, and the goal is to elicit a jailbroken (harmful) response R* at a target turn (turn 3 in the experiments). The visual prompt is a Stable-Diffusion image with a harmful key phrase typographically embedded as a caption; textual prompts escalate from a benign description request (turn 1) to a hypothetical/fictional framing request (turn 2) to an explicit request to enact the embedded harmful instruction (turn 3).
limitations: The authors' own stated future work: developing a query-optimization technique to automate adversarial prompt generation (the current attack is manually crafted/templated across 3 fixed turns), and testing on commercial reasoning-focused MLLMs and against automated jailbreaking attacks with correspondingly automated defenses. The paper itself notes that FragGuard is only demonstrated for models with text-based outputs, and that its transferability to other text-generating modalities (e.g., audio-to-text) is a conjecture, not an empirical result. Manual evaluation was limited to only 5 samples per category (65 total per model per condition), a small validation set. The defense evaluation reuses fixed hyperparameters (400-token fragment length, defender max_tokens=50, temperature=0.3) without an ablation over these choices reported in the main text. Toxicity thresholds tau and the score-4-of-5 ASR cutoff were adopted from prior work (Zhao et al. [57]) rather than independently derived.

datasets_used: MM-SafetyBench (13 OpenAI prohibited-scenario categories, 1680 total samples, 40 sampled per category for balanced evaluation; Stable-Diffusion-generated images with typographically embedded harmful key phrases)
quantitative_results: Multi-turn attack ASR turn 3: LLaVa-7B 91.50%, GPT-4o 77.31%, Gemini-2.0-Flash up to 82.3% (Table 1 / Fig 2a / text)
Attack baseline comparison on LLaVa-7B/GPT-4o (Table 1): MM-SafetyBench 72.14%/12.34%, SI-Attack 62.68%/68.57%, FigStep 62.30%/19.81%, Ours-Turn-3 91.50%/77.31%

Secondary judge set (Mistral-7B + Gemini-2.5-Flash) ASR/ATS turn2->turn3 (Table 2): LLaVa-7B 73.08%/91.35% ASR, 3.75/4.62 ATS; LLaVa-13B 24.42%/16.54% ASR, 2.30/2.17 ATS; Qwen-7B 25%/52.31% ASR, 1.98/3.34 ATS; GPT-4o 35.19%/67.50% ASR, 2.79/3.56 ATS; Gemini-2.0-Flash 43.71%/68.09% ASR, 3.04/3.58 ATS

FragGuard vs Full-Response defense, turn 3 (Table 3): LLaVa-7B attack ASR/ATS 91.50%/4.64 -> Full-Response 4.42%/1.19 -> FragGuard 0.96%/1.06; GPT-4o attack ASR/ATS 77.31%/3.82 -> Full-Response 13.46%/1.14 -> FragGuard 8.08%/1.12

Full-Response defense with Mistral-7B/Gemini-2.5-Flash judges, turn2/turn3 ASR-ATS-RR (Table 4): LLaVa-7B 19.23%/6.35% ASR, 1.88/1.26 ATS, 54.42%/85.19% RR; LLaVa-13B 15.77%/6.35% ASR; Qwen-7B 11.35%/15.00% ASR; GPT-4o 28.27%/3.50% ASR; Gemini-2.0-Flash 29.21%/35.01% ASR

FragGuard defense with Mistral-7B/Gemini-2.5-Flash judges, turn2/turn3 (Table 5): LLaVa-7B 3.85%/1.15% ASR, 1.25/1.06 ATS, 80.19%/90.00% RR; LLaVa-13B 9.04%/4.42% ASR; Qwen-7B 10%/7.31% ASR; GPT-4o 0.58%/0.00% ASR; Gemini-2.0-Flash 0.97%/0.58% ASR

Manual vs LLM evaluation (Table 6): LLaVA-7B attack ASR/ATS LLM-eval 87.69%/4.47 vs manual 89.23%/4.53; LLaVA-7B defense ASR/ATS 1.53%/1.07 (both); GPT-4o attack ASR/ATS LLM-eval 73.84%/3.73 vs manual 69.23%/3.47; GPT-4o defense ASR/ATS 6.15%/1.75 (both)

Overall summary figures (Conclusion): attack achieves up to 91.5% ASR (open-source) and 82.3% ASR (closed-source); FragGuard reduces ASR by approx. 89% for open-source and approx. 68% for closed-source models
